SFPI Access · Strategic AI Readiness

AI Readiness & Project Portfolio Dashboard

This dashboard consolidates the managerial pulse from 20 subsidiary and functional leaders across 17 separate entities. It separates the readiness diagnosis from the project portfolio, with one central message: AI deployment must be preceded by common data architecture, governance, and disciplined execution.

Shadow AI risk

Executive message

The appetite for AI is high. The execution base is fragmented. The dashboard focuses on the key facts, risks, priorities and decisions required to move from local initiatives to a governed group-level approach.

Respondents
20

Subsidiary and functional leaders involved in the managerial audit.

Separate entities covered
17

A broad cross-country view of DOM Security’s operational reality across distinct entities.

Projection density
40.0

A high signal of demand for practical AI support and cognitive load reduction.

Formal AI governance
0

No shared AI committee or common AI charter identified at group level.

Executive diagnostic

Three strategic findings emerge: high appetite, defensive use cases, and structural risk linked to fragmented data and local IT practices.

1. High appetite

Leaders want AI to reduce overload, speed up repetitive work, and regain time for management decisions.

Demand is real

2. Tactical trap

A large part of the demand is defensive: using AI to compensate for broken processes, fragmented files and legacy reporting.

Process debt

3. Shadow AI warning

Without common rules, tools and data architecture, local initiatives will multiply and increase risk instead of scaling value.

Governance gap

Current AI adoption pattern

The momentum already exists, but it is not yet structured. This creates a management choice: organize the movement or let it fragment.

Adoption signals

Individual AI usage
High
Shared AI governance
Low
Data harmonization
Weak
Cross-subsidiary scalability
Limited

Human cost of fragmentation

Operational friction translates into days lost in cross-checking, quoting, reporting and pricing validation.

Franck Chevalier · recurring time loss crossing data across multiple companies.
Jürgen Maronn · quoting workload amplified by scattered ERP, CRM and Excel data.
Céline Tamaillon · complex pricing requiring repeated back-and-forth.

Symptoms vs. root causes

AI should not simply accelerate existing disorder. It should move DOM Security from fragmented execution to governed intelligence.

Visible symptoms

  • Manual Excel consolidation.
  • Slow quoting and complex price validation.
  • Repeated administrative work across subsidiaries.
  • Informal use of public AI tools.
  • Critical know-how stored in people’s heads.

Structural root causes

  • Siloed Navision / Exact / local ERP instances.
  • No unified data dictionary or group-level APIs.
  • No common AI charter or shared usage framework.
  • Fragmented IT and business ownership.
  • Limited cross-subsidiary process standardization.

The strategic question

How does DOM Security structure a common data architecture today, so that AI becomes a driver of innovation tomorrow rather than an amplifier of existing silos?

Raw project ideas
21

Initial ideas expressed by subsidiary and functional leaders.

10

Full project hierarchy listed below, grouped by priority wave.

Defensive productivity
50%

Requests focused on internal productivity and administrative relief.

Prerequisite
Data

No scalable AI without standardized, accessible and governed data.

Demand portfolio

The project portfolio is balanced between productivity, client relations and innovation. The risk is to over-invest in tactical relief before fixing structural foundations.

Demand split

Internal productivity
50%
Client relations
30%
R&D and innovation
20%

Interpretation

Half of the demand reflects a productivity trap: leaders are asking AI to reduce the burden created by fragmented legacy systems. The other half points to real value creation: faster client response, better pricing, shorter product cycles and new service models.

Do not automate disorderScale where data is ready

Two possible trajectories

The decision is not whether to use AI. The decision is whether AI remains local and tactical, or becomes a group-level strategic capability.

Tactical relief

What happens: each subsidiary deploys local tools to save time on emails, files, quotes and reports.

Benefit: immediate productivity gains.

Risk: isolated tools, inconsistent data, duplicated costs and increased security exposure.

Short-term comfort

Strategic transformation

What happens: DOM Security builds common data architecture, governance and scalable use cases.

Benefit: durable agility, stronger control and innovation capacity across the group.

Risk: requires executive discipline and phased deployment.

Scalable advantage

Prioritized project hierarchy

The 10 projects below are ranked to separate foundation-critical initiatives from scalable business use cases. Priority is based on business impact, data dependency, cross-subsidiary relevance, adoption feasibility and time-to-value.

Foundation prerequisite before scaling

Before launching broad AI deployment, the core prerequisite is a common data architecture: harmonized data definitions, clean exports, controlled access, documented ownership and a minimum group-level governance model. This is not counted as one of the 10 use cases; it is the condition that makes them scalable.

Do firstData architectureGovernance
RankProject / use casePortfolio blockWhy this rankKey data prerequisitePriority wave
1Financial and management reporting consolidation assistantProductivityHighest immediate value because it addresses recurring cross-entity reporting workload and creates a shared data discipline.Common reporting structure, chart of accounts mapping, export standards, subsidiary ownership.Wave 1
2Automated quotation supportClient relationDirect commercial impact: compresses quotation cycles from days to minutes when pricing logic is structured.Clean product data, customer terms, validated pricing rules, margin thresholds.Wave 1
3Pricing rules standardization engineClient relationEnables faster quotes, fewer pricing errors and more consistent commercial decisions across entities.Standard pricing logic, discount rules, ERP extraction, approval workflow.Wave 1
4ERP / Navision delivery-date calculation assistantProductivityTargets a clear operational pain point and reduces manual calculations linked to legacy ERP limitations.Order data, lead times, inventory status, production constraints, delivery rules.Wave 2
5Email and attachment processing assistantProductivityReduces repetitive administrative work and improves the handling of files, requests and operational follow-up.Document taxonomy, mailbox rules, attachment types, validation workflow.Wave 2
6Client response and sales knowledge assistantClient relationImproves response speed and consistency, provided the knowledge base is validated and maintained.Approved product documentation, service policies, FAQ, commercial rules.Wave 2
7Internal knowledge capture and process documentationCross-functionalReduces dependency on tacit know-how and prepares the organization for scalable AI usage.Interview framework, process maps, validation owners, knowledge taxonomy.Wave 2
8Data quality and anomaly detection for ERP / CRM filesProductivityCreates control over fragmented data before automation; useful to prevent errors in future AI outputs.Historical ERP / CRM exports, data-quality rules, exception typology, ownership.Wave 3
9Firmware test automationR&D and innovationHigh strategic upside where manual testing slows release cycles, but requires stronger technical preparation.Structured test cases, defect history, firmware documentation, validation protocol.Wave 3
10R&D release-cycle intelligence and technical validation assistantR&D and innovationPotentially transformative for innovation speed, but should come after testing data, governance and validation standards are in place.Release history, technical requirements, test outcomes, quality gates.Wave 3

Transformation roadmap

The method requires clear ownership, structured governance and disciplined execution. Decisions, owners and deployment priorities must be defined at group level before scaling AI initiatives.

1

Executive AI & Data Steering Committee

You establish the governance body, define decision rights and stop purely individual initiatives.

2

Data Harmonization Taskforce

You map and standardize core data: pricing, reporting, supply chain, customer and product data.

3

Cross-subsidiary pilots

You select 1 or 2 pilots with visible value and sufficient data readiness before scaling further.

4

Governed deployment

You replace Shadow AI with a secure corporate toolkit, shared rules and trained leaders.

Executive mandate

Fix the foundation to unlock the intelligence. The next step is to formally convene the Executive Data & AI Steering Committee and launch Phase 1: data governance, harmonization and use-case prioritization.

Working Groups — Principles, Content & Expected Outputs

The four working groups are designed to convert the AI readiness diagnosis into practical decisions. Each group follows the same executive logic: diagnose the current risk, define the operating principles, clarify the Monday-morning actions, and produce a concrete deliverable that can be used by the 17 separate entities.

Common working principles

  • Business first: AI is assessed through operational impact, not through technical novelty.
  • Governed by design: no scalable use case without clear data ownership, rules and human oversight.
  • Local reality, group consistency: each entity keeps its constraints, but the Group defines the common frame.
  • Concrete output: each group must deliver decisions, templates, pilots or actions — not general intentions.

Expected end-of-session format

  • A short diagnostic of the starting point and current risk.
  • The key principles recommended by the group.
  • The immediate actions to launch after the session.
  • A clear deliverable: charter, pilot list, product concept, or 30-day deployment calendar.
WG-1 · Governance

The Mutualized & Localized AI Charter

This group defines the minimum common AI framework for the Group while preserving the ability to adapt locally to labour law, works councils, privacy requirements and operational practices.

Principles

  • One Group baseline.
  • Local annexes by entity or country.
  • Security, ethics and human oversight first.

Detailed content

  • Allowed and prohibited uses.
  • Data classification rules.
  • Approval and escalation workflow.

Expected output

  • 3–5 non-negotiable golden rules.
  • Local annex checklist.
  • Owner and validation path.
Decision to prepare: define which AI uses can be authorized immediately, which require review, and which must be prohibited until governance is ready.
WG-2 · Operations

Business Differentiation through Operational Performance

This group identifies how AI can improve internal execution without becoming a workaround for broken processes. The focus is on repeatable, measurable and cross-entity productivity gains.

Principles

  • Do not automate disorder.
  • Prioritize high-friction workflows.
  • Measure time saved and error reduction.

Detailed content

  • Quote generation.
  • Reporting and consolidation.
  • Customer support and administrative workflows.

Expected output

  • 2 concrete integration ideas.
  • Process map and data needs.
  • KPIs and implementation risks.
Decision to prepare: select the operational pilots that create fast value while forcing better data discipline.
WG-3 · Product

Business Differentiation through AI-Enabled Offerings

This group looks beyond internal productivity and explores how AI can become part of DOM Security’s product and service value proposition for the end customer.

Principles

  • AI must create customer value.
  • Security and trust by design.
  • Innovation must remain technically credible.

Detailed content

  • Smart access control.
  • Predictive maintenance.
  • Usage analytics or technical validation support.

Expected output

  • 2 product/service concepts.
  • Value proposition and assumptions.
  • Data, risks and roadmap needs.
Decision to prepare: identify where AI can create net-new value rather than only reducing internal workload.
WG-4 · Execution

The Restitution & 30-Day Roadmap

This group turns the seminar outputs into an executable first-month plan. Its role is to make the transition from discussion to action explicit, owned and time-bound.

Principles

  • Action over abstraction.
  • Clear owners and deadlines.
  • Small number of pilots, strong follow-up.

Detailed content

  • AI Barometer baseline.
  • Executive AI & Data Steering Committee.
  • Selection of exactly 3 priority use cases.

Expected output

  • 30-day deployment calendar.
  • Local AI Lead nomination logic.
  • Decision list with owners and KPIs.
Decision to prepare: confirm the governance rhythm, the three pilots to evaluate, and the immediate ownership model for the 17 separate entities.
